data.title: L'Oreal misses fourth-quarter expectations with 2.5% rise in sales
data.description:
French cosmetics group L'Oreal reported a 2.5% rise in fourth-quarter sales on Thursday, missing expectations due to persistently weak demand in China and a slowdown in growth in North America.

The owner of brands from Maybelline mascara to Lancome face cream said sales for the three months to end-December were 11.08 billion euros ($11.49 billion), up 2.5% on a like-for-like basis, versus expectations for a 4.4% rise, according to a consensus compiled by LSEG.

The results, slowing from a 3.4% rise in the third quarter, conclude a challenging year when a protracted slowdown in the Chinese economy and inflation in the United States dented demand for skincare and makeup in two of the company's biggest markets.

Paris-based L'Oreal also lost market share in China's mass market to domestic rivals such as Proya, analysts say, and its CeraVe and other products recommended by dermatologists face growing competition in developed markets.

Sales grew 1.4% in North America, much less than the 5.2% reported in the third quarter. In North Asia, revenues were down by 3.4%, after a 6.5% decline in the prior period.

The company said in October fourth-quarter sales growth would be comparable to the prior quarter's.

($1 = 0.9641 euros)

data.title: Toronto Stocks Flat; Bombardier Falls on Withheld 2025 Guidance, Lower 4Q Profit
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto stocks were virtually flat in midday trading Thursday.

Most sectors were leaning in red territory, led by health tech, communications and tech. Of the few gainers, process industries, consumer services and consumer durables were the leaders.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index was flat, at 25568.04, and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 edged 0.1% higher, to 1538.44.

Bombardier shares fell 4.2%, to 81.19 Canadian dollars (US$56.73), after the Canadian business-jet maker said it won't provide guidance for 2025 due to the uncertainty posed by the threat of U.S. import tariffs on Canadian goods. In its fourth quarter, the company logged a decline in profit and slower-than-expected revenue growth.

Other market movers:

Thomson Reuters shares jumped 6.1%, to C$252.11, after it said it is targeting another year of revenue growth after beating market expectations for the final quarter of 2024.

Shares of BCE Inc. fell 5.2%, to C$34.04, after it set targets for 2025 that could see revenue continue to fall due to higher competition among Canada's major telecom brands. In the fourth quarter, operating revenue fell, while profit was higher from a year earlier when it booked an asset impairment charge.

Winter-apparel company Canada Goose Holdings shares declined 5.5%, to C$14.42, after it downgraded expectations for fiscal 2025. In its fiscal third quarter, profit fell and revenue declined, missing expectations for growth.

Lightspeed Commerce's shares fell by 16%, to C$17.39, after the technology company abandoned a potential sale and said it would instead buy back shares and focus on growing its business.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Astrazeneca results, rate cut bets power FTSE 100 to record close
data.description:
Key points:
  • FTSE 100 up 1.2%, FTMC up 1%
  • AstraZeneca jumps after results
  • BoE cuts rate by 25 bps as expected
  • Growth outlook for 2025 halved, inflation forecast raised

Strong results from AstraZeneca helped London's blue-chip index notch a record close on Thursday, while a dovish majority behind the Bank of England's move to deliver a 25-basis-point interest rate cut fuelled bets of more to come.

The exporter heavy FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 also got a boost from a weaker pound, closing 1.2% higher. The domestically focussed mid-cap FTSE 250 (.FTMC) gained 1%.

The BoE cut rates to 4.5% as expected but two policymakers sought a bigger reduction.

Traders are now expecting 63 basis points in rate reductions by the end of the year from around 60 bps before the meeting.

"The tone in the minutes signals a clear easing bias for all MPC (Monetary Policy Committee) members and leaves the door open for further interest rate cuts this year," said KPMG chief economist Yael Selfin said.

"Nonetheless, domestic uncertainty remains with the upcoming tax rises and the increase in the National Living Wage."

Reaction in the pound and UK government bond market was more sombre.

Sterling slumped and UK gilt yields slipped as the BoE halved its 2025 growth outlook - a blow for finance minister Rachel Reeves who is seeking to speed up the economy.

"The difference between the FTSE 100 and the UK economy have been starkly laid bare today," said Chris Beauchamp, chief market analyst at trading platform IG.

"Any relief among individual Britons regarding lower rates is likely to be tempered by the warnings about weaker growth and higher inflation."

AstraZeneca shares jumped 6.6% to log their best day in more than 4 years as the drugmaker's update on a likely fine in China reassured investors the business impact would be minimal, while it reported fourth-quarter earnings slightly above analysts' estimates.

Anglo American was also among the top FTSE 100 gainers.

Most major subsectors were in the green pharma and biotech index (.FTNMX201030) jumping 3.7% and industrial metal miners (.FTNMX551020) rising 3%, helped by 5% gain in Anglo American after its fourth-quarter production report.

Highlighting the economic challenges, British construction firm activity dropped in January with housebuilding contracting at its fastest rate in a year.

Elsewhere, a survey showed British households' expectations for inflation fell in January, with one-year expectations falling to 3.5% from 3.7%.

data.title: Swiss Stocks Extend Gains; Leonteq Shares Sink
data.description:
Swiss stocks remained in positive territory on Thursday as investors assess the UK central bank's monetary policy decision.

The Swiss Market Index closed 0.36% higher at the end of the trading day.

Switzerland's unemployment rate edged up to 3% in January from 2.8% in the previous month, government data showed. The number of unemployed people increased by 5,480 to 135,773, compared with the month before.

Zooming out to the UK, the Bank of England lowered its bank rate by 25 basis points to 4.5%. The Monetary Policy Committee voted 7-2 in favor of the cut, compared with market expectations of an 8-1 vote. Inflation is expected to rise "quite sharply" to 3.7% in the third quarter, partly due to energy prices, before easing again toward the BoE's 2% target.

"In line with our view, the BoE delivered a dovish twist to its guidance with a dovish vote split, which triggered the immediate market reaction. Yet the statement and press conference revealed that BoE still favors a 'gradual' and 'careful' approach to easing monetary policy," Danske Bank said in a note.

Over to corporates, Swiss banking software systems company Temenos added 1.16% following news that it agreed to sell its Multifonds business to Montagu Private Equity for an enterprise value of $400 million, including an earnout, as part of plans to simplify its product portfolio. The deal is expected to complete in the second quarter.

On the flip side, Leonteq shares plunged 11.06% as it reported a year-over-year drop in 2024 group net profit to 5.8 million francs from 20.6 million francs, dragged down by a 41% fall in net trading result amid continued low market volatility. The Swiss fintech's net fee income, meanwhile, rose 1% to 214.4 million francs.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0740 ET - Technology and healthcare are the sectors expected to benefit the most from artificial intelligence, according to a survey of entrepreneurs released by UBS. Two thirds of the 156 surveyed entrepreneurs see AI as the technology that offers the greatest commercial opportunity for their industries, the report says. Expectations for AI benefits are lower among industrial entrepreneurs, the report says. In five years, more than two thirds of those surveyed expect the technology to lead to improved productivity for a typical company in their industry, it says. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0710 ET - German chip maker Infineon Technologies will continue to outperform rivals amid market-share gains in semiconductors found in cars, Stifel's Juergen Wagner writes in a note to clients. Infineon's results for its fiscal first quarter to the end of December show that the company is an industry outperformer, Wagner says. This is particularly true in automotive, he notes, since Infineon continues to gain market share in microcontroller units and silicon-carbide semiconductor products for cars. The company also has a strong foothold in the competitive Chinese electric-vehicle market, he says. Infineon shares trade 3.8% higher at 36.85 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0600 ET - Texas is constructing more houses than any other state in the U.S., Realtor.com reports, highlighting the Lone Star State's appeal to newcomers, driven by housing, jobs, and climate. More than 25% of home shoppers are from out of state, with significant migration from California and internationally. Texas leads in new home permits, accounting for 15% of the 2024 U.S. total despite representing only 9% of the population. The state excels in affordable housing, with new homes shrinking by 5.3% since 2020 to 2,073 square feet, compared to a 3.6% national reduction. Texas's median home price of $360,000 in December 2024 remains $40,000 below the national median. Affordability challenges remain, as 51% of Texans earn under $75,000 annually, with only 17% of homes affordable to them. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0439 ET - Qualcomm shares slip premarket as investors weigh a number of factors outside of the chip maker's control despite strong results for its fiscal first quarter, JPMorgan analysts write in a note to clients. Investors are concerned about the company losing business with Apple should the iPhone maker replace Qualcomm's chips in some phone models with in-house versions, the analysts say. Geopolitical headwinds, including the expiration of an agreement with Huawei, is also weighing on investors' minds, they say. Qualcomm stock closed 1.6% higher at $175.86 ahead of results on Wednesday. Shares are down 4% premarket, touching a low of $167.94. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0337 ET - WeChat's e-commerce system could gain stronger awareness among consumers in 2025 through a broader adoption of its gifting function, Citi analysts say in a research note. The Tencent-owned popular messaging app in December rolled out a gifting function, which has successfully expanded the consumption scenarios within the app's ecosystem during the Lunar New Year, the analysts note. Citi predicts the function to be more widely used, thanks to the app's "social graph" alongside a growing number of participating merchants. Citi maintains a buy rating on Tencent, with its target price unchanged at HK$563.00. Shares of Tencent last ended at HK$420.40. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0303 ET - U.S. tariffs will likely have a minimal impact on software companies, while hardware companies will face a bigger hit, Janus Henderson Investors says in a commentary. Software isn't reliant on physical asset production and has a geographical distributed workforce, whereas hardware is often manufactured abroad and relies on components such as semiconductor chips and wafers. The components are mainly produced in Asia-Pacific and then shipped to Mexico for assembly to take advantage of lower labor costs, the asset manager adds. "The impact of the recently announced tariffs would therefore be most pronounced in the assembly phase." Janus Henderson expects the impact on large end-market manufacturers like Dell and HP to be more acute than their suppliers. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

0006 ET - The magnitude of price cuts in China's electric-vehicle industry this year should be much less than last year, Jefferies analysts write in a note. Since inventories at BYD dealers are much lower and the profitability of new car sales is much better, the analysts reckon BYD is unlikely to kick off an aggressive price war like last year. Among the top Chinese EV makers, Xiaomi, Huawei-backed Harmony Intelligent Mobility Alliance and XPeng are outperformers by new order intakes, they note. The introduction of Tesla's full-self driving features in China this year could serve as a good example for its Chinese rivals on how carmakers can commercialize their advanced assisted driving technologies, they add. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2301 ET - Chinese startup DeepSeek's reasoning model could significantly cut AI training and inference costs, help AI technology expand and diversify AI applications, HSBC Global Research analysts write in a note. The adoption of DeepSeek-R1 could help software companies improve research and development efficiency, save costs and boost earnings growth in the medium term, they add. DeepSeek's reportedly cheap yet powerful model could help boost the monetization of AI applications, especially in the Chinese market, they add. The brokerage expects AI agents to contribute significant revenues to software companies early in 2H. AI is also likely to be a revenue driver for leading software players amid China's soft macroeconomic conditions, it says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2248 ET - Investors should take a selective approach to the Malaysian technology sector, focusing on stocks with clear strategies, long-term vision, and earnings visibility through secured orders, says AmInvestment Bank Analyst Paul Yap Ee Xing in a note. Equipment makers offer the best risk-reward, supported by stable earnings forecasts, a recovering order book, and trends in supply chain relocation, he reckons. While high earnings expectations pose a risk, Yap still sees value in the sector, given low investor holdings and strong long-term growth potential. As AI and data center trades unwind, investors seeking to rebalance their portfolio may return, he adds. AmInvestment Bank maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's technology sector and pegs Vitrox Corp. and VS Industry as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2245 ET - SGH remains a buy for UBS analysts, who anticipate ongoing earnings momentum from the Australian engineering and construction group. They think that the stock's recent rerate to 20 times earnings on a one-year forward basis, compared with a 10-year average of 13X, has been underpinned by investors' greater appreciation of its proven earnings delivery and investment discipline. With operational discipline at the Boral business now under SGH's control and mining maintenance demand looking strong at its WesTrac unit, they forecast average annual earnings growth of 8% for the next three fiscal years. UBS raises its target price 24% to A$56.00. Shares are up 0.7% at A$49.32. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1251 ET - Snap's 4Q beat and optimistic outlook were encouraging, say analysts at BofA Securities in a research note, but user growth remains limited in high value US/EU markets. The analysts also say the app transition rollout suggests mixed results, and that big EBITDA upside seems less likely given growing expenses. BofA says Snap's stock-based compensation expense exceeds the investment bank's free cash flow estimates through 2027. Snap slides 7%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

data.title: Paysafe Stock Jumps on Report of Takeover Interest — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

Shares of Paysafe surged Thursday following a report that said the online payment service company was considering a sale after receiving takeover interest.

Shares rose 18% to $23.36 after Bloomberg reported that Paysafe was exploring its options with a financial advisor. The report cited people familiar with the matter.

Paysafe went public in March 2021 following a $9 billion merger with a blank-check company headed by businessman Bill Foley. The company is backed by Blackstone Group and CVC Capital Partners.

A Paysafe representative didn't immediately respond to a request for comment from Barron's.

This is breaking news. Check back for updates soon.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1052 ET - Siemens Healthineers 1Q beat and a realistic 2025 outlook are another step in the right direction, Barclays analysts say in a note. The German healthcare-equipment company's sales strength in the Americas in its imaging, and particularly in its computed tomography and molecular business, supports a healthy capex environment in the U.S. and share gains for Siemens Healthineers over the next 1-3 years, they say. Shares are up 6.4% at 57.54 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1018 ET - The health system in the U.K. needs to develop a more attractive investment environment, AstraZeneca's chief executive officer Pascal Soriot says in a media call. High taxes and access issues have created problems for the entire industry, he says. The British pharma giant last week withdrew plans to invest in a vaccine plant in Liverpool after what it called a cut in government support. Shares are up 5.8% at 117.70 pounds. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0932 ET - Eli Lilly is slightly higher is early trading after another blowout quarter driven by GLP-1 drugs. 4Q revenue rises 45% to $13.53 billion. Revenue from new products climbs by $3.15 billion to $5.64 billion, driven Zepbound and Mounjaro. Lilly's fortunes echo those of Danish rival Novo Nordisk, which sold$4.71 billion of its Ozempic drug in 4Q. Lilly shares have more than quintupled in the last five years. Rival drug makers, such as Novartis, have admitted hunting for obesity drugs of their own. Lilly and Novo have major first-mover advantage, with smaller players such as Viking Therapeutics still playing catchup. Anya Schiess, a partner at JPMorgan's asset management unit, is among those who have predicted GLP-1s will be the best-selling class of drugs yet. (rob.curran@dowjones.com)

0838 ET - Siemens Healthineers could exceed its full-year outlook after it posted a sales and profit beat and better-than-expected China results, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. The German healthcare-equipment company's 1Q results are a "best-case outcome", exceeding consensus expectations by 2% on both sales and adjusted EBIT, and 9% on adjusted EPS, they say. Its business in China declined by 7%, a fall that was less steep than expected, and should soothe investor concerns, they say. Shares are up 6% at 57.54 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0824 ET - AstraZeneca's higher spending on research and development is a positive and crucial for it to build out its pipeline, UBS analysts say in a note. The British pharma giant generated higher-than-expected collaboration income, which it reinvested in R&D spending that grew 23% in the fourth quarter, the analysts say. "This is in line with UBS thesis that Astra Zeneca needs to increase R&D spend to prosecute the rich pipeline," UBS says. This year is key for the drugmaker, with seven expected late-stage trial results due, they say. Shares are up 5.8% at 117.70 pounds. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0740 ET - Technology and healthcare are the sectors expected to benefit the most from artificial intelligence, according to a survey of entrepreneurs released by UBS. Two thirds of the 156 surveyed entrepreneurs see AI as the technology that offers the greatest commercial opportunity for their industries, the report says. Expectations for AI benefits are lower among industrial entrepreneurs, the report says. In five years, more than two thirds of those surveyed expect the technology to lead to improved productivity for a typical company in their industry, it says. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0622 ET - AstraZeneca's sales beat and key catalysts for 2025 outshine its core operating profit miss, Barclays analysts says in a note. The British pharma giant's oncology drugs beat sales expectations, driven by lung-cancer drug Tagrisso and breast-cancer treatment Lynparza, Barclays says. However, its core operating profit margin missed company-compiled consensus by 30%, they say. "We see AstraZeneca as having the strongest catalyst path in 2025 of any company in our coverage," the analysts say. Shares are up 5.1% at 116.92 pounds. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0618 ET - AstraZeneca expects earnings and sales to grow this year, which makes its midterm target seem achievable, Interactive Investor analyst Richard Hunter says. High demand for the British pharma company's cancer drugs lifted sales in the fourth quarter while seven key late-stage clinical results this year set the company on the right footing to reach its midterm sales target, the analyst says. "The 2030 aim of $80 billion of revenues looks ambitious yet increasingly achievable, with AstraZeneca still confident of hitting the target given the strength of its late and mid-stage pipelines," he says in a market comment. Shares are up 5.1% at 116.92 pounds. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0601 ET - AstraZeneca's China troubles seem manageable, Quilter Cheviot healthcare analyst Sheena Berry says in a note. The British pharma company facing potential charges from an import tax allegation against some of its employees in China creates a slight overhang but entails an amount that it can handle, the analyst says. "China has proven to be an overhang on the stock following the fraud investigations of several individuals, and questions remain on exactly what impact this might have on operations in China," she says. Shares are up 5.2% at 117.02 pounds. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1206 ET - The cloud of possible tariffs hangs over Canada's banks. Morningstar DBRS says if U.S. tariffs are implemented as originally devised, the impact would be negative for Canadian banks and credit unions. That would reverse expectations asset quality for the sector will stop deteriorating in 2H. Still, while certain banks and credit unions are more exposed than others, capital positions are robust, and the banking regulator and federal and provincial governments have support options, Morningstar says. "Although a tariff war would produce a much more challenging macroeconomic and operating environment for the Canadian banking sector in 2025, Canadian banks generally remain well positioned with adequate liquidity, stable funding and sound capital levels," says Morningstar's Carl De Souza. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

1105 ET - Currently, 17.2% of U.S. homeowners with mortgages have an interest rate at or above 6%, up from 12.3% in 3Q 2023, per Redfin. If this trend persists, this figure could nearly double in three years. Meanwhile, 82.8% enjoy rates below 6%, fueling the "lock-in effect" where homeowners avoid selling due to higher current rates. However, this effect is easing; the share with rates below 6% was 87.7% in 3Q 2023 and a high of 92.7% in mid-2022. The housing shortage is easing as more homes are listed, partly due to people moving for life changes like job shifts or divorce, and growing acceptance that low rates won't return soon. Also, many with significant home equity or no mortgage are not rate-bound, contributing to increased listings. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1048 ET - New listings U.S. home for sale surged 7.9% year-over-year for the period ending Feb. 2, marking the largest increase since the end of last year, according to Redfin. But, despite a slight uptick in pending sales from the previous month, they're still down 8.1% annually. This has led to a 5-month supply of homes on the market, up from 4.4 months a year earlier, the most in six years. Homes are now selling for 2% below their list price, the biggest discount in nearly two years. However, buyer hesitation persists due to near-record high home prices and mortgage rates, with median monthly payments at $2,784, just $21 below the peak. Additionally, cold weather and economic policy uncertainty are deterring potential buyers. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0642 ET - Monte dei Paschi reported a mixed set of fourth-quarter results that come at an uncertain time for the bank in the context of its recent bid to take over rival Mediobanca, ING's Suvi Platerink Kosonen says. "The bid for Mediobanca creates some uncertainty although we see a limited likelihood of success due to the relatively low offer," the analyst writes in a research note. As for its results, the Italian bank's top line was broadly stable after net interest income--the difference between what it earns on loans and pays on deposits--fell under pressure from lower interest rates, ING says. In 2025, bank guides for a pretax profit at least in line with that of 2024. Shares fall 0.7%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0624 ET - Societe Generale's outlook is reassuring and the French bank seems to be making progress with a tweak to its payout policy to boost returns to shareholders, Citi analysts say in a research note. The lender ended 2024 with a healthy capital position, partly benefiting from recent asset sales, Citi says. "This stronger starting point, plus higher future organic capital generation should continue to support capital return," Citi says. Societe Generale plans to pay out half of its net profit going forward, up from 40%-50% previously. Alongside fourth-quarter results, the bank declared a dividend that is marginally lower than anticipated and a buyback of 872 million euros that is significantly higher than expected, Citi says. Shares jump 9.9%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0335 ET - The Bank of Japan should raise interest rates to at least above 1% if economic growth continues on greater flexibility in the job market and productivity improvements, says Masamichi Koike, deputy president and executive officer at Japanese megabank Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corp. However, given that the country still hasn't completely overcome a deflationary mindset, "there may be a shock [to the economy] if the BOJ raises rates too rapidly," he says at an event in Tokyo. Meanwhile, Japan's corporate sector will likely witness more consolidations and bankruptcies this year as the economic recovery spurs healthy competition, he says. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0322 ET - Max Financial Services' life-insurance unit seems well placed to achieve its FY 2025 guidance, Nomura analysts say in a research report. For the unit, management guides for 20% annual-premium-equivalent growth and high-single-digit growth for value of new business in FY 2025, the analysts note. Management appears reasonably confident that the unit can post a VNB margin of 23%-24% in FY 2025, the analysts say. With the effects of India's surrender value regulations for the insurance industry now mostly factored in, the company appears well on course to deliver on the guidance, the analysts add. Nomura upgrades the stock to buy from neutral and raises the target price to INR1,300.00 from INR1,250.00. Shares are 1.0% lower at INR1,107.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0310 ET - Hannover Re's confirmation of its guidance for 2025 is positive in light of the Los Angeles wildfires, which have been a clear drag on the reinsurance sector year to date, analysts at JPMorgan say. The German reinsurer didn't provide an estimate on the impact of the California fires, but it did reaffirm its previous guidance for net profit of 2.4 billion euros this year. It also confirmed earnings for last year at 2.3 billion euros, in line with previous guidance and said it achieved premium growth of 7.6% at its January treaty renewals with pricing reduced 2.1% on a risk-adjusted basis. "Price reductions for Hannover Re take profitability back to 2023 levels which was a strong baseline in our view," JPM says. Shares rise 0.3%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0219 ET - Societe Generale's better-than-expected results and guidance for 2025 that points to more cost controls should provide confidence that its 2026 targets are in reach, RBC Capital Markets' Anke Reingen and Matthew Russell say in a research note. The French lender delivered a good set of results, the analysts say. An increase to its payout ratio to half of net profit from 2024 onward isn't totally surprising, RBC says. Moreover, Societe Generale is targeting a return on tangible equity--a key profitability metric--of more than 8% in 2025 and reiterated its guidance of a return of 9%-10% next year. This should give confidence on its outlook, given that consensus expectations are close to the low end of its 2025 guidance but remain below Societe Generale's 2026 targets, RBC says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

2352 ET - Bank Mandiri (Persero)'s earnings growth is likely to stay stable, Maybank Sekuritas Indonesia analysts say in a research report. The Indonesian bank's net interest margin has shown signs of recovery in 4Q by widening 9bps on quarter, and is likely to continue expanding, the analysts say. The bank's healthy credit quality together with its high non-performing loan coverage of over 250% should keep its credit costs stable going forward, the analysts add. Maybank Sekuritas Indonesia maintains a buy rating but lowers the stock's target price to IDR7,675.00 from IDR8,000.00 partly because the bank's 2024 earnings missed the brokerage's estimates. Shares are 7.2% lower at IDR5,125.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1926 ET - Pinnacle's bull at Wilsons Advisory believes that its opportunity in Australia's managed-accounts market has yet to be fully priced into its stock. Analyst Cameron Halkett tells clients in a note that he has conviction in the medium-term EPS growth potential from managed accounts offered by Pinnacle affiliate Pacific Asset Management. He points to Life Cycle's strong performance since becoming an affiliate in Pinnacle's most recent fiscal half as evidence of the opportunity on offer. It's possible that Pinnacle could deliver more than A$100 million in gross performance fees in its fiscal second half, Halkett adds. Wilsons lifts its target price 9% to A$28.95 and stays overweight on the stock, which is down 1.7% at A$25.60. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1900 ET - Computershare could beat its full-year EPS guidance amid slower-than-expected interest-rate cuts in the U.S., Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. They lift their forecasts for so-called management EPS by 3% in fiscal 2025 and by 6% in fiscal 2026, citing U.S. rates and better margin balances. The Australia-listed share-registry provider benefits from higher rates due to interest earned on funds held for dividends. Morgan Stanley now anticipates 10% growth in fiscal 2025 management EPS, which is about 3% ahead of company guidance. MS lifts its target price by 12% to A$31.10 and stays equal-weight on the stock, which is up 1.1% at A$35.17. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0950 ET - Oil futures are recouping some of the previous day's losses, while the market continues to ponder the impact of trade tariffs, rising U.S. crude stocks, and prospects of a step-up in OPEC+ output in April. "Most traders feel the U.S. tariff threats to China are for real and could damage their economy further, and with OPEC set to raise production soon, WTI prices may have heavier headwinds in the future," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "Look for a choppy type trade until more is known about tariffs." WTI is up 0.7% at $71.53 a barrel, and Brent is 0.6% higher at $75.02.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0840 ET - Suncor Energy logged a strong 4Q performance that caps off an exceptional year and reinforces a bullish medium- to longer-term outlook, RBC Capital Markets' Greg Pardy says. Adjusted funds from operations per share came in 6% higher that the analyst expected and the consensus forecast, while total production was in line. Suncor affirmed 2025 guidance that points to production of 810,000-840,000 bbl/day. RBC has an outperform call and C$65 target on the shares, which last closed at C$55.44. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0834 ET - Suncor Energy's 4Q adjusted funds from operations came in 10% above the expectations of Raymond James, despite the Canadian company pre-releasing production and refining throughput figures. Analyst Michael Barth says the beat seems to largely be driven by lower corporate costs. He notes Suncor's 2025 guidance was unchanged. Barth expects the market to react positively to the results, given the renewal of is share buyback program is anticipated in late-February and Suncor has ample capacity to start the new program strong. Raymond James has a market perform stance on shares, which are up 8% in 2025 and 29% over the last 12 months. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0509 ET - Orsted's cut to capital expenditure combined with its commitment to sell 70 billion-80 billion Danish kroner in assets is likely to bring reassurance on the balance sheet and credit metrics, UBS's Mark Freshney writes. However, a 10% rise in costs on a like-for-like basis is a negative for the Danish renewable-energy company, he adds. UBS says it would appear that the spending cuts relate to delaying or avoiding new projects between 2026 and 2030, so the EBITDA impact is long term. The full-year 2025 EBITDA guidance is very close to the bank's forecasts and only 3% below its forecasts for 2026. "Scaling back the business given cost pressures and no equity raise is a positive surprise." Shares trade 5.9% higher at 297.70 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0431 ET - Petronas may face a lower financial impact than initially expected following a recent policy shift in Sarawak's gas sector, Citi analyst Steven Chan says in a note. Sarawak's state-owned Petroleum Sarawak will now manage only domestic gas supply, while LNG exports remain under Petronas's purview. Concerns had risen that Petronas's revenue and free cash flow could be affected as Petroleum Sarawak became the sole gas aggregator in 2H 2024. Chan notes that LNG exports account for 94% of Sarawak's natural gas output and 27% of Petronas's 2023 revenue. He views the development as positive, easing concerns over potential upstream capital expenditure cuts by Petronas and ensuring the Malaysian state-owned energy company retains exclusive control over exploration and distribution with no disruptions to existing agreements. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0404 ET - Orsted's 2025 Ebitda guidance of 25 billion to 28 billion Danish kroner is in line with consensus at 27.1 billion kroner, RBC Capital Markets analysts Alexander Wheeler and Ziyad Jasimuddin write. Gross investment guidance of 50 billion to 54 billion is also in line with the 52.5 billion expected by RBC. The company yesterday announced it was dropping its 2030 EBITDA and installed capacity targets, while reducing its gross investments to 2030 by around 25%. "We think that the decision to reduce the investment targets will be more of a focus on the earnings call as the market looks to Orsted's balance sheet and what impact this may have." Shares rise 5.5% to 296.30 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0049 ET - Global Power Synergy's earnings may be impacted by a potential electricity tariff cut in Thailand, CGS International's Amornrat Cheevavichawalkul says in a research report, as the brokerage lowers its target price to THB32.50 from THB43.50. The Energy Regulatory Commission of Thailand has proposed a reduction in electricity tariff to THB3.98/unit, versus the current THB4.15/unit, by removing the adder tariff and feed-in-tariff from the electricity cost structure, the analyst notes. The brokerage estimates that every THB1/unit reduction in the electricity tariff should lead to a 12.8% decline in the Thai power company's 2025 EPS. The brokerage maintains its hold rating as concerns over potentially lower tariffs should mostly be priced in. Shares are 5.9% lower at THB28.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0012 ET - The scenario for Brent crude oil at $60-$65 per barrel through 2H just got stronger, Citi Research's Francesco Martoccia says in a research report. An increase in non-OPEC+ oil supply remains poised to outpace weak global demand growth from 2Q through 4Q, with the oil market shifting into surplus, the energy strategist says. "We remain strongly of the view that President Trump could ultimately prove to be a bearish influence on the oil market," Martoccia says. He notes that Trump has consistently highlighted lower energy prices as the central solution to issues such as U.S. inflation. Front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.1% higher at $74.69/bbl; front-month WTI crude oil futures are 0.2% higher at $71.18/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2313 ET - Thailand's utilities sector is likely facing major headwinds, Thanachart Securities' Nuttapop Prasitsuksant says in a report. The brokerage downgrades the sector to underweight from overweight. Headwinds include expiring favorable legacy contracts, slow bidding for new projects, and escalating policy risks around tariff rate reductions, the analyst says. The brokerage's new top pick is Gulf Energy Development, as factors like its strong growth outlook seem secured by in-the-pipeline projects with upside from more M&A. Thanachart Securities raises the stock's target to THB65.00 from THB54.00 and maintains a buy rating. Shares are 0.4% higher at THB57.75. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2150 ET - Keppel Infrastructure Trust's acquisition of a 50% equity interest in Marina East Water looks positive, OCBC Investment Research's Ada Lim says in a note. The bank maintains the trust's buy rating. The acquisition entitles the trust to full economic benefit from MEW, which owns Keppel Marina East Desalination Plant. The acquisition is expected to be accretive to unitholders, as the plant is a high-quality asset that will generate long-term stable cash flows, Lim says. The nature of this asset is also aligned with the trust's portfolio and deepens exposure to water treatment in Singapore, she adds. However, OCBC trims the trust's fair value estimate to S$0.500 from S$0.510 to reflect factors including a higher risk-free rate. Units are unchanged at S$0.450. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1812 ET - Comet Ridge's signal that a final investment decision on its Mahalo natural-gas project in Australia won't happen this year contributes to Bell Potter cutting its price target by 19% to A$0.21/share. Still, analyst Stuart Howe remains bullish about Comet Ridge, highlighting that Mahalo is one of the few development-ready projects capable of delivering near-term gas supply to eastern Australia. Comet Ridge expects preliminary design and engineering work at Mahalo to continue through this year. Only when this is completed will it make a final investment decision, or FID, with partner Santos. "We expect Comet Ridge's majority equity positions in the Mahalo projects and a strong gas market outlook to provide support for development funding across debt, equity and gas prepayments," Bell Potter says. "However, we also acknowledge risks to FID timing." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1744 ET - Beach Energy's interim dividend falls short of market expectations, with Barrenjoey noting the payout doesn't align with the company's distribution policy. Beach declared an interim dividend of 3 Australian cents a share. While that was up 50% on last year it fell short of the 4 cents anticipated by the market. Analyst Dale Koenders points out that Beach reported free cash flow of A$431 million in 1H. "But this free cash flow then also implies a payout ratio of only 16%, below policy of 40-50%," Koenders says in his initial response to the result. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0955 ET - Aurubis' first quarter is likely to have been the strongest period of fiscal 2025, Warburg analyst Stefan Augustin says in a research note. The German supplier of non-ferrous metal and recycler of copper stated that the first quarter was positively influenced by a significantly higher metal result, more than compensating for a year-over-year drop in treatment and refining charges. The anticipated lower treatment and refining charges will be felt from next quarter onward, Augustin says. Copper concentrate treatment and refining charges--also known as TC/RCs--are paid by mining companies to smelters. Shares trade 5.1% higher at 78.50 euros. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0955 ET - Natural resources companies should weather the tariff storm if imposed, Morningstar DBRS suggests. Although U.S. tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico have been delayed, the threat remains. Morningstar says there would be short-term disruptions in trade flows until supply chains are rebalanced if the levies are imposed. Yet it doesn't expect mining companies to scale back production and would instead deliver on operational guidance provided for 2025. The robust commodity prices that have persisted over the past few years have allowed mining companies to significantly reduce debt levels and consequently they have strong credit metrics, Morningstar says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0947 ET - Steelmaker ArcelorMittal says it intends to build a plant in Alabama to produce the paper-thin steel used in the motors of electric vehicles, air conditioners and other products. Construction of the $1.2-billion plant at ArcelorMittal's steel mill near Mobile, Ala., will start later this year. Production is projected to begin in 2027. The plant will be able to produce about 165,000 tons a year. Production of electric vehicles is expected to drive higher demand for electrical steel, which accounts for about 1% of the worldwide steel production annually with most of it now in Asia. The only U.S. producers of electrical steel are Cleveland-Cliffs and U.S. Steel, which recently began production in Arkansas. (robert.tita@wsj.com )

0840 ET - Suncor Energy logged a strong 4Q performance that caps off an exceptional year and reinforces a bullish medium- to longer-term outlook, RBC Capital Markets' Greg Pardy says. Adjusted funds from operations per share came in 6% higher that the analyst expected and the consensus forecast, while total production was in line. Suncor affirmed 2025 guidance that points to production of 810,000-840,000 bbl/day. RBC has an outperform call and C$65 target on the shares, which last closed at C$55.44. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0834 ET - Suncor Energy's 4Q adjusted funds from operations came in 10% above the expectations of Raymond James, despite the Canadian company pre-releasing production and refining throughput figures. Analyst Michael Barth says the beat seems to largely be driven by lower corporate costs. He notes Suncor's 2025 guidance was unchanged. Barth expects the market to react positively to the results, given the renewal of is share buyback program is anticipated in late-February and Suncor has ample capacity to start the new program strong. Raymond James has a market perform stance on shares, which are up 8% in 2025 and 29% over the last 12 months. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0653 ET - Anglo American investors have focused on the fact that fourth-quarter copper production was ahead of expectations rather than the weak pricing, RBC Capital Markets analysts Ben Davis and Marina Calero write. Prices were generally below consensus, they write. Copper pricing missed expectations by 4%, while iron ore pricing missed by around 7%, they add. The cut to diamond production was more than expected as Anglo hopes to put a floor under diamond prices, the analysts write. Shares trade up 6.5% at 2483.5 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0647 ET - Anglo American's preparations to write down the value of its De Beers unit shows why it is so keen to jettison the business, says AJ Bell's investment director Russ Mould. The London-listed miner cut 2025 diamond production guidance as it looks to stabilize supply while clearing its large inventory, he says. De Beers cut prices by 10% to 15% in its last auction of 2024 as demand faltered, he says. It remains to be seen whether Anglo American is vulnerable to a bid from a rival after it fought off interest from BHP last year, he adds. Shares trade up 6.5% at 2483.5 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0406 ET - Anglo American reported a decent fourth-quarter production update, Barclays analysts write. Its key iron ore, copper and platinum group metals divisions were broadly ahead of consensus. However, sales volumes were mixed while realized prices were weaker than expected, they write. Anglo's medium-term copper guidance is unchanged but new 2027 guidance of 760,000 to 820,000 tons is lower than expected, they write. Shares trade up 4.4% at 2435.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2305 ET - Posco Holdings is set to post weaker-than-expected 2025 earnings from its steel and battery-material businesses, Nomura analyst Cindy Park writes in a note. Park cuts her net-profit forecast for the South Korean steelmaker by 48% this year. She sees a lukewarm business outlook for Posco's steel and battery-material businesses in 2025 with steel demand expected to rise just 1.2% globally and to fall 1.0% in China. It may take time before Posco's asset restructuring makes a meaningful earnings contribution, she adds. Nomura downgrades its rating on the stock to neutral from buy and cuts its target price by 46% to KRW270,000. Shares are 0.6% lower at KRW240,500. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2145 ET - Palm oil prices rise in early Asian trading, tracking the gains in soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. Expectations of weaker palm oil output further support CPO prices, he adds. Malaysia's January palm oil output is expected to have declined 10% from the previous month to 1.34 million tons, a Bloomberg survey shows. Ng pegs CPO support at 4,300 ringgit/ton and resistance at 4,450 ringgit/ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for April delivery is 9 ringgit higher at 4,343 ringgit/ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1739 ET - Zircon prices are likely to remain resilient, benefiting Australia's Iluka Resources, says Macquarie. It expects the zircon price to stay above US$1,730/ton in the medium term and then rise in 2029 when the market tips into a deficit. "We also highlight that our supply forecast does not include production rationalization by major producers, which happened during the last zircon price trough in 2016," Macquarie says. Demand trends look positive. The continued push by the Chinese government to ensure property developments are completed supports demand for ceramics, which represents some 50% of zircon consumption, Macquarie says. It keeps an outperform call on Iluka. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1715 ET - Despite a tough farm economy in 2024, Corteva expects improvements this year. "We acknowledge the fluid macro environment but still see ag market fundamentals improving - specifically, we see continued record demand for core crops and recovering grain prices and farm margins," CEO Chuck Magro said in a statement. Shares of the agriculture company ticked down 6% during post-market trading after it said sales rose 7% to about $4 billion in its latest quarter, slightly below analyst expectations for the period. Corteva, one of the largest sellers of crop seeds and chemicals in the world, and other agriculture companies faced a tough agriculture market this past year because of declining grain prices and rising costs. Corteva said sales volumes for its pesticides and seeds both were up double digits for the quarter that ended Dec. 31 compared with the prior year as prices for its products ticked down. (patrick.thomas@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1532 GMT - Over the past decade, North American carmakers have remade their supply chains for light-vehicle combustion engines as they strategize about an electric-vehicle future, economists at the Chicago Fed report. Light-vehicle plants in North America sourced 60 different types of internal-combustion engines in 2016, but that fell to 35 in 2023, representing a serious streamlining. "We interpret this as an outcome of carmakers' efforts to manage the transition toward electrification while maintaining profitability of their portfolio of vehicles featuring internal combustion engines," the economists, Thomas Klier and James Rubenstein, write. Somewhat fewer engines are now shipped across borders now than in 2016 as well, perhaps slightly lessening the impact of new North American tariffs that Trump has threatened. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1455 GMT - European defense stocks tumbled between 2% and 3% on Thursday, after Bloomberg reported on Wednesday that U.S. allies expect President Donald Trump's administration to present a ceasefire plan for the Ukraine-Russia war at the Munich Security Conference in Germany next week. Sweden's Saab fell 4.6% in afternoon trade to 227.6 Swedish krona. German defense stocks experienced some of the biggest falls, with Rheinmetall decreasing 4.4% to 716 euros, sensor solutions provider Hensoldt falling 4% to 37.74 euros, and RENK Group slipping 3.4% to 23.3 euros. Citi analysts Sam Burgess and Charles Armitage say any ceasefire deal is likely to be on terms more favorable to Russia than Ukraine, and European governments will see a clear need for credible European deterrence if Russia remains bellicose after a ceasefire. That should support the shares longer-term. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1247 GMT - Volvo Car has reported weak underlying fourth-quarter results with 2025 earnings guided lower, Jefferies analysts write. The company expects a "turbulent 2025" with lower volumes and profitability with the probability of higher discounts across the industry as well as higher depreciation and amortization, Jefferies notes. Free cash flow is expected to be positive on cash preservation, despite peak capital expenditure, but the order book is back to normal pre-Covid levels, the bank adds. Shares fall 9.8% to 21.38 kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1210 GMT - German chip maker Infineon Technologies will continue to outperform rivals amid market-share gains in semiconductors found in cars, Stifel's Juergen Wagner writes in a note to clients. Infineon's results for its fiscal first quarter to the end of December show that the company is an industry outperformer, Wagner says. This is particularly true in automotive, he notes, since Infineon continues to gain market share in microcontroller units and silicon-carbide semiconductor products for cars. The company also has a strong foothold in the competitive Chinese electric-vehicle market, he says. Infineon shares trade 3.8% higher at 36.85 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - Volvo Car's earnings consensus is unlikely to change much and UBS doubts investors will factor in more positive 2025 free cash flow guidance so early in the year due to tariff uncertainty, lower inventories and volume growth volatility. The company reported fourth-quarter sales and EBIT excluding joint ventures that beat consensus, while free cash flow is also slightly better than guided. This year's guidance is for no volume growth and no profitability improvement, while free cash flow guidance has been raised and the 2026 outlook confirmed. "Volvo ended 2024 with a healthy level of inventories. We like the fact that it is taking a pragmatic approach on volume growth and EBIT outlook," analyst David Lesne writes. Shares trade 8.9% lower at 21.58 Swedish kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0953 GMT - A.P. Moeller-Maersk said it is launching a $2 billion buyback but there are questions over the logic of returning extra cash to shareholders, Bernstein analysts Alex Irving and Tobias Fromme say. This is especially so given the improving security situation in the Bab-el-Mandeb strait as the Houthis have, for now, ended their ban on almost all shipping activity, the analysts say. Although the shipping company has high levels of liquidity and cash reserves, so do its competitors, the analysts say in a note. The liners with the strongest balance sheets will be best placed to weather the storm, the analysts add. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0932 GMT - Maersk's share buyback of up to $2 billion announced late Wednesday should provide optimism given recent uncertainties over the disruptions in the Red Sea, Barclays analysts say. Added to that, the Danish shipping company has set its targets for 2025, with Ebitda guidance of between $6 billion and $9 billion, compared with Barclays's estimates of $6.5 billion, they say in a note. Maersk's 2025 EBIT is also in line with expectations, and global container volume is guided to grow at around 4%, they add. However, the decision to launch the buyback with guidance in line means that Barclays is likely to use nearly all of its cash buffer for 2025, meaning there will be little to no cash for acquisitions if the buyback is completed, the analysts add. Shares are up 7.8% at 11,715 Danish kronor.(michael.susin@wsj.com)

0930 GMT - A.P. Moeller-Maersk's investors are likely to focus on the timing of its return to the Suez Canal, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. This is especially so after several vessels of rival shipping company MSC were seen crossing through the Red Sea, the analysts say. Furthermore, the Danish firm's Ocean division seems to be heading for a loss in the second half based on management guidance for 2025, they say. EBIT is expected between $0 and $3 billion, and Ebitda between $6 billion and $9 billion, dependent on the timing of the Red Sea reopening, they say. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0545 GMT - Samsung Heavy Industries is expected to exceed its 2025 guidance for new contract wins, Nomura analyst Eon Hwang writes in a note. Hwang expects new orders for the South Korean shipbuilder to jump 59% to $12 billion this year, beating its own target of $9.8 billion. Samsung Heavy's operating profit is projected to rise 54% in 2025, Hwang says. He is also upbeat on Samsung Heavy bidding for two floating liquefied natural gas projects this year--one off Mozambique and the other in the U.S. Nomura raises its target price for the stock by 14% to KRW16,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 1.8% lower at KRW13,160. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0506 GMT - The magnitude of price cuts in China's electric-vehicle industry this year should be much less than last year, Jefferies analysts write in a note. Since inventories at BYD dealers are much lower and the profitability of new car sales is much better, the analysts reckon BYD is unlikely to kick off an aggressive price war like last year. Among the top Chinese EV makers, Xiaomi, Huawei-backed Harmony Intelligent Mobility Alliance and XPeng are outperformers by new order intakes, they note. The introduction of Tesla's full-self driving features in China this year could serve as a good example for its Chinese rivals on how carmakers can commercialize their advanced assisted driving technologies, they add. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0405 GMT - Toyota Motor's plan to build a new Lexus factory in Shanghai to develop and produce electric vehicles and batteries is a positive for China's auto sector, Daiwa analyst Kelvin Lau writes in a note. The newly formed entity in Shanghai will develop an EV model under the Lexus brand, with production to start from 2027. The initial production capacity will be around 100,000 units annually. The plan reflects Toyota's commitment to the Chinese EV market, and implies opportunities for the carmaker's existing and potential partners, Lau says. Daiwa remains cautiously positive due to limited details regarding the Shanghai factory, but it's an important step for Toyota to achieve its 2035 electrification goal. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0314 GMT - Yangzijiang Shipbuilding's management is expected to guide for higher new order wins this year, says UOB Kay Hian analyst Adrian Loh. The shipbuilder's new Yangzi Hongyuan yard in China will be ready for operation in 2H 2026, he writes in a note. With the new capacity taken into consideration, the brokerage estimates Yangzijiang Shipbuilding's new order win target could be between US$6 billion and US$7 billion. The company has a 2024 order win target of US$4.5 billion. UOB Kay Hian has a buy rating on the stock and a target price of S$3.60. Shares are flat at S$3.07.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: SUBURBAN PROPANE PARTNERS LP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Suburban Propane Partners LP, a leading distributor of propane, fuel oil, and related products and services, has released its Form 10-Q report for the first quarter of fiscal 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial and operational performance, highlighting key metrics and challenges faced during the period.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenues: $373.3 million, reflecting a 2.0% increase compared to the prior year first quarter.

Gross Margin: $226.2 million, increased by $13.4 million or 6.3% compared to the prior year first quarter, driven by a $3.6 million unrealized gain from derivative instruments.

Operating Income: $59.1 million, up from $48.7 million in the prior year first quarter, indicating improved operational efficiency.

Net Income: $19.4 million, down from $24.5 million in the prior year first quarter, impacted by lower heat-related demand and agricultural demand.

Net Income Per Common Unit - basic: $0.30, compared to $0.38 in the prior year first quarter, reflecting the decrease in net income.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The propane segment remains the largest contributor to revenue, with significant sales to residential, commercial, industrial, government, and agricultural customers. The fuel oil and refined fuels segment, as well as the natural gas and electricity segment, also contribute to the overall revenue, albeit to a lesser extent.

Geographical Performance: The company experienced varied demand across its service territories, with the Southeast region seeing increased demand following Hurricanes Helene and Milton. However, overall demand was impacted by unseasonably warm temperatures, particularly in November, which was one of the warmest on record.

Sales Units: Retail propane gallons sold in the first quarter of fiscal 2025 were 105.7 million gallons, a slight decrease of 0.8% compared to the prior year, primarily due to lower heat-related demand and reduced agricultural demand for crop drying.

Operational Challenges: The company faced inflationary pressures affecting the costs of goods and services, including labor, transportation, and capital expenditures. These factors, along with competition for resources, have impacted operational costs and consumer demand.

Future Outlook: Management anticipates continued challenges from inflation and supply chain pressures but remains focused on customer base growth and retention initiatives to drive future performance. The company is also monitoring regulatory changes in New York that could impact its natural gas and electricity business.

SEC Filing: SUBURBAN PROPANE PARTNERS LP [ SPH ] - 10-Q - Feb. 06, 2025

data.title: Air Products & Chemicals, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Air Products & Chemicals, Inc., a leading global industrial gases company, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of the fiscal year. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance and operational activities, reflecting the challenges and opportunities faced in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Sales: $2,931.5 million, a decrease of 2% from the prior year due to lower volumes and unfavorable currency impact, partially offset by favorable pricing.

Gross Margin: 31.2%, an increase of 20 basis points from the prior year as pricing and favorable business mix more than offset the impact of higher costs.

Operating Income: $643.6 million, a decrease of 3% due to higher costs and lower volumes, partially offset by positive pricing and favorable currency.

Net Income: $649.8 million, an increase of 5% as higher pricing and lower non-service pension costs were partially offset by higher costs.

Diluted EPS: $2.77, an increase of 1% from the prior year, reflecting higher pricing and a gain on de-designated cash flow hedges.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments

  • Americas: $1,287.6 million
  • Asia: $817.1 million
  • Europe: $697.2 million
  • Middle East and India: $32.8 million
  • Corporate and other: $96.8 million

Sales were primarily driven by on-site and merchant supply modes, with a notable sale of equipment in the Corporate and other segment.

Geographical Performance

The Americas segment experienced a significant, non-recurring sale of helium to an existing merchant customer, which partially offset lower volumes in other regions. The Europe segment faced lower contributions from on-sites and merchant supply modes, while the Asia segment maintained stable performance.

Sales Units

The company experienced a 2% decrease in sales volumes, primarily due to the divestiture of the LNG business and lower contributions from on-sites and merchant supply modes in Europe. However, this was partially offset by a significant helium sale in the Americas.

New Production Launches

The NEOM Green Hydrogen Project in Saudi Arabia is a key focus, with ongoing construction and financing activities. The project is expected to significantly contribute to future operational capacity and revenue streams.

Future Outlook

Management anticipates realizing significant benefits from the U.S. Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, particularly from Section 48 energy investment tax credits, once qualifying projects come onstream. The company is also evaluating the impact of new accounting guidance and climate-related disclosure requirements on future operations.

SEC Filing: Air Products & Chemicals, Inc. [ APD ] - 10-Q - Feb. 06, 2025

data.title: Weekly Jobless Claims Rise; January Job Cuts Increase Sequentially
data.description:
Weekly applications for unemployment insurance in the US rose more than expected, according to government data, while a separate report showed that job cuts in the country increased sequentially in January.

The seasonally adjusted number of initial claims increased by 11,000 to 219,000 in the week ended Feb. 1, the Department of Labor reported Thursday. The consensus was for a 213,000 level in a survey of analysts compiled by Bloomberg. The previous week's reading was revised up by 1,000 to 208,000.

The four-week moving average came in at 216,750, advancing by 4,000 from the prior average that was revised up by 250. Weekly unadjusted claims climbed by 11,370 to 239,690.

"Despite the modest increase in the week ended (Feb. 1), initial claims for unemployment insurance benefits remain low and not appreciably higher than this time last year," Oxford Economics Chief US Economist Ryan Sweet said in remarks emailed to MT Newswires. "Initial claims can be choppy but the four-week moving average remained comfortably below our estimate of its break-even level, or that consistent with no monthly job growth."

Seasonally adjusted continuing claims totaled 1.89 million for the week ended Jan. 25, ahead of the Bloomberg consensus of about 1.87 million. Continuing claims jumped by 36,000 from the previous week's level, which was revised down by 8,000. The four-week moving average came in at 1.87 million, increasing by 2,250 from the prior week's downwardly revised average, according to the DOL.

"Continuing claims for unemployment insurance — a proxy for hiring — resumed its increase in the week ended (Jan. 25)," according to Sweet. "This is consistent with decline in the job finding rate and the rise in duration of unemployment."

On Friday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics is expected to report that the US economy added 170,000 nonfarm jobs in January, which would mark a slowdown from the 256,000 gain posted for the month prior, according to a Bloomberg poll.

US-based employers cut 49,795 jobs last month, up 28% from December, but down 40% annually, according to a separate report released Thursday by Challenger Gray & Christmas. This was the lowest January layoffs total since 2022. Among industries, the technology sector lost the most number of jobs.

"January was relatively quiet in terms of job cut announcements," Andrew Challenger, senior vice president of Challenger Gray & Christmas, said. "However, we've already seen major announcements in the early days of February, so it seems this quiet is unlikely to last."

data.title: Qualcomm and Arm Can Gain From DeepSeek. They Need 1 Thing to Change. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

Qualcomm and Arm Holdings executives are the latest to say the prospect of low-cost artificial intelligence is a good thing. The two chip companies have a decent argument, but they need sales of AI smartphones to take off.

Practically every chip and technology company to report results this earnings season has said that DeepSeek's demonstration of low-cost AI development is actually a good thing for the sector, even though a panic about the Chinese startup's advances wiped away nearly $1 trillion of value from the U.S. stock market.

Qualcomm, a specialist in chips for mobile phones, and the semiconductor-design company Arm have a better argument than most. Both companies should benefit from more computing moving from the cloud onto devices, a way of operating known as "edge AI." More efficient AI models bring that future closer.

Qualcomm CEO Cristiano Amon told analysts on an earnings call Wednesday that DeepSeek models were running on smartphones and personal computers powered by the company's chips within a few days of its release. "DeepSeek-R1 and other similar models recently demonstrated that AI models are developing faster, becoming smaller, more capable and efficient, and now able to run directly on device," Amon said.

That is a shift that can't come quickly enough for Qualcomm, whose stock performance has trailed far behind peers such as Nvidia that provide chips to power AI data centers. The hoped-for boost to smartphone sales that edge AI could bring has been slow to arrive, and handset-related revenue makes up the majority of Qualcomm's business.

In Qualcomm's favor, its handset revenue rose 13% in the first quarter. That is way above the 2.4% overall growth in global phone shipments in the last quarter of 2024, as estimated by International Data Corporation.

"Like Apple, Qualcomm is seeing an uptick in its premium tier with those handset makers that introduced AI-related features. However, the impact of this positive trend could be a little more muted in 2H25 as we prepare for the loss of Apple content in the September quarter and beyond," wrote Melius Research analyst Ben Reitzes in a research note.

Beginning this fall, Apple will begin to substitute 5G chips of its own making for those made by Qualcomm.

Reitzes kept a Hold rating on Qualcomm stock but raised his target price to $190 from $180. Qualcomm shares were down 4.9% at $167.28 in morning trading.

Meanwhile, Arm CEO Rene Haas told analysts that DeepSeek's demonstration of cheaper AI was "terrific" for the company in its own earnings call.

"As wonderful a product as [Nvidia's] Grace Blackwell [chip] is, you'd never be able to put it in a cellphone, you'd never be able to put it into earbuds. You can't even put it into a car. But Arm is in all those places," Haas said.

Arm isn't quite as dependent on smartphones as Qualcomm. Although it does plenty of business licensing its designs for handsets, it also has a growing position in high-end cloud-server processors and in AI-enabled PCs. Still, more AI smartphone sales would promote the use of Arm's latest advanced chip technology, called Armv9, which generates double the royalty rates of the previous version.

"We...view the emergence of AI Agents and efficient AI models (such as DeepSeek) as a significant positive for ARM given its strong presence at the edge," wrote Raymond James analyst Srini Pajjuri in a research note.

Pajjuri raised his target price on Arm to $175 from $160 and kept an Outperform rating on the stock.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

data.title: Urals diffs stable, freight rates in Russia's East ease
data.description:
Urals crude differentials were steady on Thursday, while freight rates for Russia's oil loading from Far East Kozmino port eased amid higher availability, traders said.

Freight rates for ESPO Blend crude shipped from the Russian port of Kozmino to China have fallen 50% in early February from mid-January levels as a shortage of tankers following U.S. sanctions on the fleet shipping Russian oil eased, four traders said.

Freight rates for Russian oil loading from its western ports remained relatively stable as availability of the cargoes was good, traders said.

Traders expected Azeri BTC oil loading plan for March to be released on Friday.

PLATTS WINDOW

  • No bids or offers were shown for Urals, Azeri BTC and CPC Blend in the Platts window on Thursday.

NEWS

  • China's retaliatory tariffs on the United States may cause U.S. oil exports to decline in 2025 for the first time since the COVID-19 pandemic, after growth plateaued last year.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1208 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper up 0.2% at $9,283.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum flat at $2,624.50 a ton. Base metals are holding ground as investors await further clarity around trade tensions, with copper up 2.25% on-week and aluminum up just 0.1%. Metals were pressured lower on Monday after U.S. President Trump imposed tariffs on Canada, Mexico and China, before bouncing back later in the session after the Mexican and Canadian tariffs were put on hold for one month. The Chinese tariffs remain in place, and China has vowed to retaliate with tariffs of its own with an implementation date of Feb. 10. This economic uncertainty has damped investor appetite for industrial metals, as tariffs will be negative for global economic growth. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1052 ET - U.S. natural gas inventories declined by 174 billion cubic feet last week, in line with the five-year average for the period, the EIA reports. At 2,397 Bcf, gas in underground storage at the end of January was 111 Bcf below the five-year average and 208 Bcf below the year-ago level. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey had forecast a 170 Bcf drawdown for last week. Nymex natural gas is off 1.2% at $3.319/mmBtu, supported on the downside by some colder weather foreseen for mid-February. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1019 ET - Live cattle futures are down 1.8% in early trading, bringing the most-active CME contract down below the $2/lb mark. Fund traders have been net long on cattle due to the expectations of tight supply versus strong demand, but these traders appear to be cutting their exposure to long positions as uncertainty due to tariff threats and other initiatives out of the Trump administration. Longer-term, cattle prices are expected to keep rising amid strong demand for U.S. beef overseas and light slaughter rates domestically. Lean hog futures are down 0.4% in early trading. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1009 ET - Hershey said its volume of chocolate sales decreased in its most recent quarter, which CEO Michele Buck attributed in part to convenience stores facing pressure. Consumers also continue to be focused on health and wellness, she said, with the chocolatier seeing growth in zero-sugar and protein products. Successful innovation in the sweets category has also contributed, Buck said, highlighting Shaq-a-licious sour gummies and freeze dried Jolly Ranchers as two recent examples, and will come to Reese's Peanut Butter Cups later this year. (roshan.fernandez@wsj.com)

0955 ET - Aurubis' first quarter is likely to have been the strongest period of fiscal 2025, Warburg analyst Stefan Augustin says in a research note. The German supplier of non-ferrous metal and recycler of copper stated that the first quarter was positively influenced by a significantly higher metal result, more than compensating for a year-over-year drop in treatment and refining charges. The anticipated lower treatment and refining charges will be felt from next quarter onward, Augustin says. Copper concentrate treatment and refining charges--also known as TC/RCs--are paid by mining companies to smelters. Shares trade 5.1% higher at 78.50 euros. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0955 ET - Natural resources companies should weather the tariff storm if imposed, Morningstar DBRS suggests. Although U.S. tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico have been delayed, the threat remains. Morningstar says there would be short-term disruptions in trade flows until supply chains are rebalanced if the levies are imposed. Yet it doesn't expect mining companies to scale back production and would instead deliver on operational guidance provided for 2025. The robust commodity prices that have persisted over the past few years have allowed mining companies to significantly reduce debt levels and consequently they have strong credit metrics, Morningstar says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0950 ET - Oil futures are recouping some of the previous day's losses, while the market continues to ponder the impact of trade tariffs, rising U.S. crude stocks, and prospects of a step-up in OPEC+ output in April. "Most traders feel the U.S. tariff threats to China are for real and could damage their economy further, and with OPEC set to raise production soon, WTI prices may have heavier headwinds in the future," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. "Look for a choppy type trade until more is known about tariffs." WTI is up 0.7% at $71.53 a barrel, and Brent is 0.6% higher at $75.02.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0949 ET - Cocoa alternatives are in growing demand given high prices for the real thing, Hershey CEO Michele Buck said. Some companies feeling the pressure have switched to cocoa butter alternatives, she said, reducing demand in the global cocoa market. "We do that where possible, but we're pretty precious about the brands and what they stand for with consumers, " Buck said. (roshan.fernandez@wsj.com)

0948 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are lower ahead of the EIA's weekly storage report, but holding ground with some colder weather returning to mid-February forecasts. Natural gas storage likely fell by 170 Bcf to 2,401 Bcf in the last week of January, according to a Wall Street Journal survey, leaving stocks at a 107 Bcf deficit to the five-year average. The Nymex front month is down 1.3% at $3.317/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0947 ET - Steelmaker ArcelorMittal says it intends to build a plant in Alabama to produce the paper-thin steel used in the motors of electric vehicles, air conditioners and other products. Construction of the $1.2-billion plant at ArcelorMittal's steel mill near Mobile, Ala., will start later this year. Production is projected to begin in 2027. The plant will be able to produce about 165,000 tons a year. Production of electric vehicles is expected to drive higher demand for electrical steel, which accounts for about 1% of the worldwide steel production annually with most of it now in Asia. The only U.S. producers of electrical steel are Cleveland-Cliffs and U.S. Steel, which recently began production in Arkansas. (robert.tita@wsj.com )

0907 ET - Arabica coffee futures rise to a record high on supply worries. ICE coffee futures rose 1.7% to around $4.05 a pound in European afternoon trading. They have climbed as high as $4.11 earlier in the session. The recent price rises reflect persistent supply concerns, especially in Brazil, ING analysts say in a note. There are suggestions that market conditions will remain tight as exchange inventories continue to fall, while the next harvest is set to suffer from bad weather, ING says. Adverse weather prior to October deteriorated harvest prospects for the crop, and market participants have reported bleak forecasts. Coffee futures have notched successive highs in nearly every session since mid-January. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0900 ET - Western brands lost sales in their stores doing business in the Middle East after the outbreak of the Israel-Hamas war in October 2023, but some are seeing improvements. Fast food owner Yum Brands says transactions in its KFC restaurants in the Middle East rebounded in 4Q compared to before the conflict, with same-store sales in the region up 21%. Yum is up 3% premarket on a slight adjusted 4Q EPS beat. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)
